pathophysiology_summary: Allergic rhinitis is IgE-mediated nasal mucosal inflammation on aeroallergen exposure (here renovation dust), with mast-cell histamine release producing paroxysmal sneezing, watery rhinorrhoea, nasal itch and congestion, and frequently allergic conjunctivitis. Turbinates appear pale/boggy (versus erythematous in viral rhinitis). It is benign; the subjective 'suffocating' sensation reflects nasal obstruction, not lower-airway or laryngeal compromise. The ground-truth task is (1) allergic vs viral discrimination (itch, sudden allergen-triggered onset, no prodrome/fever, pale boggy turbinates), (2) exclusion of anaphylaxis/laryngeal oedema and asthma (no wheeze, patent airway), and (3) correct step-up management: intranasal corticosteroid as maintenance mainstay, oral antihistamine, allergen avoidance, and topical decongestant limited to <=3 days.

differential.evidence_basis: Overlaps but lacks itch, has prodrome/fever and erythematous (not pale) turbinates.
Screen for wheeze/lower-airway involvement given breathing-distress framing.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Allergic rhinitis leads; anaphylaxis and asthma must be actively excluded before reassurance.

differential.evidence_basis: Itch-predominant, allergen-triggered, no prodrome/fever, no wheeze/airway compromise — allergic cause confirmed; anaphylaxis and asthma excluded.
No airway/systemic features; escalation target if they develop.
Itch and sudden allergen onset without prodrome favour allergic over viral.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After negative anaphylaxis/asthma screen, allergic rhinitis leads; intranasal corticosteroid + antihistamine + avoidance.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; benign, controlled with avoidance and step-up pharmacotherapy.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is benign allergic rhinitis.

red_flags.description: Anaphylaxis / airway compromise — throat tightness/closing, lip/tongue swelling, stridor, wheeze, difficulty swallowing
red_flags.clinical_significance: Distinguishes benign nasal obstruction from life-threatening laryngeal oedema/bronchospasm.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating subjective nasal-obstruction panic as impending airway emergency without screening airway/lower-airway features.
Prescribing antibiotics for a non-infective allergic process.
Recommending oxymetazoline without a strict <=3-day cap (rebound congestion).

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Step-up allergic-rhinitis therapy + avoidance; call 000 for throat tightness/swelling/wheeze.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Missing anaphylaxis/asthma behind nasal-obstruction panic; rebound congestion from prolonged decongestant.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Intranasal corticosteroids are the most effective maintenance therapy; add oral/intranasal antihistamine and allergen avoidance. Topical decongestants only short-term (<=3–5 days).
